content.text: What It Is

Error handling and retry standards define how an AI system responds when operations fail. For systems that call LLM APIs, orchestrate multi-step agentic workflows, or depend on external tools, failures are not exceptional events — they are routine. Rate limits, transient network issues, model overload responses, and occasional provider-side errors are predictable characteristics of production AI systems. Standards ensure that every failure is classified, handled consistently, and routed through the right recovery path.

The core of these standards is a three-class error taxonomy: transient errors that warrant automatic retry, persistent errors caused by invalid request construction that should be routed to a dead letter queue for inspection, and fatal errors that require immediate alerting and human intervention. Layered on top of this taxonomy are concrete implementation patterns: exponential backoff with jitter to avoid thundering herd on retry, idempotency requirements to ensure retried operations do not cause double-execution side effects, and structured error response formats that carry enough context for observability tooling to correlate failures across distributed service calls.
Why It Matters

Without explicit error handling standards, individual engineers make ad-hoc retry decisions: retry once, retry three times with a fixed delay, or not at all. These inconsistent strategies produce unpredictable behaviour under load. A service that retries five times on a 429 response with a 100ms fixed delay adds significant amplification to an already-stressed provider API. A service that does not retry at all treats a transient network hiccup as a permanent failure and propagates the error to the user unnecessarily.

The idempotency requirement is particularly critical for agentic AI systems. When a tool-use call succeeds on the provider side but the response is lost due to a network partition, a naive retry will execute the tool twice. If that tool is a database write, an email send, or an API call to an external payment system, duplicate execution has real consequences. Idempotency keys allow the receiving system to detect and suppress duplicates, making retry safe.

Structured error responses matter for observability. When an error from a downstream LLM API is surfaced to a client with no additional context — just a 500 status code — the on-call engineer has no information to distinguish a rate limit from an authentication failure from a model-side context overflow. A structured error response that includes the error type, the original request ID, and a retry-after hint allows observability tooling to classify the failure and route the alert to the right responder.
Benefits
Reduced unnecessary failures.
Transient errors that would terminate a user-visible operation with a retry policy are transparently recovered, improving the success rate of LLM API calls without changes to the calling application.
Thundering herd prevention.
Exponential backoff with jitter staggers retry timing across concurrent callers, reducing the correlated load spike that would occur if all callers retried simultaneously after a 503 response.
Safe retry semantics.
Idempotency keys make retry safe for operations with side effects. The system can retry confidently without risking duplicate actions.
Faster incident diagnosis.

Structured error responses with correlation IDs reduce mean time to diagnosis for LLM API failures. The error type field alone determines whether the response is a code bug (persistent) or an infrastructure issue (transient).
Predictable dead letter behaviour.
Operations that cannot be retried are consistently routed to a dead letter queue with full payload context, enabling human review and reprocessing rather than silent data loss.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Error taxonomy and retry decision flow: API calls that succeed go to Success; errors are classified as Transient (exponential backoff retry) or Persistent/Fatal (dead letter queue). After max retries, transient errors also route to DLQ.
attrs.title: Error Taxonomy and Retry Decision Flow

content.text: The decision flow starts at every API call. A successful response goes directly to the caller. An error response is classified into one of three categories. Transient errors enter the exponential backoff loop with configurable max attempts. Persistent and fatal errors bypass the retry loop entirely and go directly to the dead letter queue. When a transient error exceeds its retry budget, it also routes to the dead letter queue, where it can be inspected and manually reprocessed once the underlying condition is resolved.
Error Taxonomy
Transient Errors

Transient errors are caused by temporary conditions that are expected to resolve without changes to the request. The canonical transient error for LLM API callers is HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests), which indicates that the caller has exceeded a rate limit. HTTP 503 (Service Unavailable) and 502 (Bad Gateway) indicate that the provider's infrastructure is temporarily overloaded. Network-layer transient errors include connection timeout, read timeout, and connection reset.

The defining property of a transient error is that retrying the identical request after a delay has a meaningful probability of success. The delay is not arbitrary: exponential backoff with jitter is the correct strategy because it gives the stressed system time to recover while avoiding the correlated retry spike that a fixed delay would create.
Persistent Errors

Persistent errors are caused by problems with the request itself. HTTP 400 (Bad Request) typically indicates malformed JSON or missing required fields. HTTP 422 (Unprocessable Entity) is used by several LLM providers to indicate semantic validation failures: prompt exceeds the context window, a required parameter has an invalid value, or the model identifier is unknown. Retrying a persistent error without changing the request will always produce the same result. These errors must be routed to the dead letter queue for inspection by the team that owns the calling service.
Fatal Errors

Fatal errors indicate configuration or security problems that require immediate human intervention. HTTP 401 (Unauthorized) means the API key is missing or expired. HTTP 403 (Forbidden) means the caller does not have permission to use the requested model or endpoint. HTTP 404 (Not Found) usually indicates a misconfigured base URL or a model ID that does not exist. These errors should trigger an alerting event immediately; they will not resolve without a configuration change.
Exponential Backoff with Jitter — Implementation

The retry decorator below implements full jitter exponential backoff. The delay for attempt n is: min(base_delay * 2^(n-1), max_delay) plus or minus a random fraction of that delay. Full jitter is preferred over equal jitter because it produces better load distribution across many concurrent retrying callers. The decorator is exception-type-selective: it retries on TransientError only, and re-raises PersistentError and FatalError immediately.
Dead Letter Queue Implementation

The dead letter queue implementation captures the full payload and error context for every non-retryable failure. The message schema is versioned and includes all fields required for reprocessing: the original payload, the error type, the attempt count, and a correlation ID. The DLQ implementation below targets AWS SQS FIFO queues, which provide exactly-once delivery semantics and ordered reprocessing within a message group.

import json
import uuid
import logging
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Any

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@dataclass
class DeadLetterMessage:
    original_request_id: str
    error_type: str
    error_message: str
    http_status_code: int
    timestamp_utc: str
    service_name: str
    operation_name: str
    original_payload: dict
    attempt_count: int

class DeadLetterQueue:
    def __init__(self, sqs_client, queue_url: str, service_name: str):
        self._sqs = sqs_client
        self._queue_url = queue_url
        self._service_name = service_name

    def enqueue(
        self,
        operation_name: str,
        error: Exception,
        payload: dict,
        attempt_count: int,
    ) -> str:
        msg = DeadLetterMessage(
            original_request_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
            error_type=type(error).__name__,
            error_message=str(error),
            http_status_code=getattr(error, "status_code", 0),
            timestamp_utc=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
            service_name=self._service_name,
            operation_name=operation_name,
            original_payload=payload,
            attempt_count=attempt_count,
        )
        response = self._sqs.send_message(
            QueueUrl=self._queue_url,
            MessageBody=json.dumps(asdict(msg)),
            MessageGroupId=self._service_name,
            MessageDeduplicationId=msg.original_request_id,
        )
        logger.error(
            "operation_routed_to_dlq",
            extra={"msg_id": response["MessageId"], "operation": operation_name},
        )
        return response["MessageId"]
Idempotency Requirements

Every operation that is eligible for retry must be idempotent. Idempotency means that executing the same operation multiple times produces the same result as executing it once. For LLM API calls that have no side effects (pure inference), idempotency is automatic. For operations that write to a database, send notifications, or call external APIs, idempotency must be explicitly implemented.
Generate a UUID v4 idempotency key before the first attempt. Store the key in the request context, not as a function parameter, so it persists across retry iterations.
Send the key in the X-Idempotency-Key header with every attempt, including retries. The provider or downstream service uses this key to detect duplicate requests.

For services that do not natively support idempotency keys, implement a deduplication store in Redis with a TTL equal to the maximum retry window. Before executing the operation, check whether the key already exists in the store. If it does, return the cached result. If it does not, execute the operation, store the result, and return it.
Set the deduplication store TTL to at least the maximum cumulative retry delay plus a safety margin. For five attempts with max_delay of 60 seconds, a 300-second TTL is a safe minimum.
Log the idempotency key with every attempt so that the deduplication store entry can be found during incident investigation.
YAML Error Handling Standard

# error-handling-standard.yaml
# Enterprise AI Engineering — Error Handling and Retry Standard v1.2

error_taxonomy:
  transient:
    description: >
      Errors caused by temporary infrastructure conditions. The operation
      may succeed if retried after a delay.
    http_status_codes: [429, 500, 502, 503, 504]
    network_conditions:
      - connection_timeout
      - read_timeout
      - connection_reset
    retry_policy:
      strategy: exponential_backoff_with_jitter
      base_delay_seconds: 1.0
      max_delay_seconds: 60.0
      jitter_factor: 0.25
      max_attempts: 5

  persistent:
    description: >
      Errors caused by invalid request construction. Retrying without
      changing the request will always fail. Route to dead letter queue
      for inspection.
    http_status_codes: [400, 422]
    retry_policy:
      strategy: none
      action: dead_letter_queue

  fatal:
    description: >
      Errors indicating authentication, authorisation, or resource
      configuration problems. Immediate alerting required.
    http_status_codes: [401, 403, 404]
    retry_policy:
      strategy: none
      action: alert_and_dead_letter_queue

idempotency:
  requirement: mandatory_for_all_retried_operations
  implementation:
    - header: X-Idempotency-Key
      format: uuid_v4
      ttl_seconds: 86400
  verification:
    - providers_that_support: [stripe, anthropic_batch, openai_responses]
    - custom_dedup_store: redis
      key_pattern: "idempotency:{service}:{idempotency_key}"

dead_letter_queue:
  backend: aws_sqs           # or gcp_pubsub, azure_service_bus
  retention_days: 14
  alert_on_enqueue: true
  required_message_fields:
    - original_request_id
    - error_type
    - error_message
    - http_status_code
    - timestamp_utc
    - service_name
    - operation_name

structured_error_response:
  schema:
    error:
      code: string           # machine-readable, e.g. "RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED"
      message: string        # human-readable summary
      error_type: string     # transient | persistent | fatal
      request_id: string     # trace correlation ID
      retry_after_seconds: integer | null
      details: object | null # optional extended context
Structured Error Response Format
Common Anti-Patterns
Retry on 400.
HTTP 400 errors indicate malformed requests. No amount of retrying will fix a missing required field. Retrying 400s wastes quota and adds latency without benefit.
Fixed-delay retry.
Retrying with a fixed delay does not give the system time to recover from overload. Exponential backoff is required. Fixed delays are acceptable only for non-production test environments.
Retrying without idempotency.

Any operation with side effects that is retried without an idempotency key risks duplicate execution. The risk is highest for tool-use calls in agentic workflows where the agent may retry after a context switch.
Swallowing persistent errors.
Catching PersistentError, logging it, and returning a default value hides bugs that need developer attention. Persistent errors must flow to the dead letter queue where they are visible to the team.
No jitter.

Exponential backoff without jitter synchronises retry storms. If 100 callers hit a 503 simultaneously and retry after exactly 2 seconds, they all hit the system again at exactly the same moment. Jitter randomises the retry window.
Key Takeaways
Classify every error as transient, persistent, or fatal before deciding on a retry strategy. The classification determines everything that follows.
Exponential backoff with full jitter is the correct retry strategy for transient errors. Base delay 1s, max delay 60s, max 5 attempts covers the majority of LLM API rate-limit scenarios.
Idempotency keys are mandatory for any retried operation with side effects. Generate the key before the first attempt and send it with every retry.
Dead letter queues must capture the full request payload and error context. An alert fires on every DLQ enqueue so that persistent errors do not silently accumulate.
Structured error responses that include error type and retry-after hints allow observability tooling to distinguish transient from persistent failures and route alerts correctly.
Fatal errors (401, 403) require immediate alerting and must not be retried. They indicate configuration problems that a retry cannot fix.
